<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714395 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00826: Method specification report

- page_id: `136714395`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714395/Method+specification+report
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:31.025+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Use Case driven
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The purpose of the Method Specification report is to show the method design and its related use cases in detail to developers.

The report shows a class diagram and a list of all classes in the diagram. The list contains attributes and methods for each class. The report also shows the method specification, parameters, and algorithms.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The purpose of the Method Specification report is to show the method design and its related use cases in detail to developers.</p><p>The report shows a class diagram and a list of all classes in the diagram. The list contains attributes and methods for each class. The report also shows the method specification, parameters, and algorithms.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711981 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00827: Metric Suites

- page_id: `136711981`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711981/Metric+Suites
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:03.360+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1497238846

1497238847

1895909674

**On this page**

4

1497238847

A metric is the item of information about the specific aspect of a model. The collection of various metrics can be used to evaluate the current state of the model. Calculated at regular intervals they can help to track the evolution of model development.

800450

For better understanding of further material, get acquainted with the following concepts

| Concept | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Metric Suite | A collection of parameter and metric definitions used to calculate the metrics that evaluate specific aspects of your model. |
| Parameter definition | The variable that is used to calculate metrics. It can be an element type (including both relationship and DSL element types), particular element, data type, or data type value. |
| Metric definition | The attribute of a Metric Suite defining the formula for calculating metrics according to the parameter values. |
| Validation-based metric definition | The attribute of a Metric Suite specifying the validation rule for calculating metrics according to the parameter values. |

In general, a Metric Suite is a Class with the «MetricSuite» stereotype applied. Both parameter and metric definitions are attributes of a Metric Suite.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

One metric is the result of a single calculation of a metric definition. The metrics of all the metric definitions of a Metric Suite together with relevant parameters are stored in a single instance of a Metric Suite (as slot values). To analyse and manage instances of a Metric Suite with a modeling tool, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metric table' space='MD2024x'].

##### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
Creating Metric Suites

There are several conventions that you must know about Metric Suites before creating one:

- Metric Suite must have a target. A target specifies model elements, for which metrics of the particular metric suite can be calculated. The target value can be any type of elements or even a particular element of your model.
- Metric Suite must have one parameter definition declared as context. The element, which is selected for calculating metrics, is automatically set as the value of the context parameter definition.
- Metric Suite must have a date tracker, so that you could see the timestamp of each metric.

To create and define a Metric Suite, complete the following steps:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a new Metric Suite' space='MD2024x'] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specify the target for the Metric Suite' space='MD2024x'] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying parameter definitions' space='MD2024x'] (including the context parameter definition).
4. Build [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Building metric definitions' space='MD2024x'] and/or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Building validation-based metric definitions' space='MD2024x'] .

It’s recommended to have a single person per project, who is responsible for creating and managing all the Metric Suites of the project.

1497238846

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specific use cases' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710381 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00828: Metric table

- page_id: `136710381`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710381/Metric+table
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T14:41:42.344+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

222736122

222736125

222736121

**On this page**

222736124

##### **Description**

This feature is available in Architect and Enterprise editions only.

The Metric Table is one of four MagicDraw tables. The Metric Table allows you to collect various metrics used to evaluate the current state of your model. A Metric is an item of information about a specific aspect of the model. Each row in the table represents a set of metrics calculated according to the metric suites specified by the user.

To understand the material better, you need to be familiar with the following concepts.

| Concept | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Metric suite | A collection of parameter and metric definitions used to calculate metrics that evaluate specific aspects of your model. |
| Parameter definition | A variable used for calculating metrics. It can be an element type (including both relationship and DSL element types), a particular element, data type, or data type value. |
| Metric definition | A formula for calculating metrics according to the parameter values. |
| Validation-based metric definition | The attribute of a Metric Suite specifying the validation rule for calculating metrics according to the parameter values. |

Actually, a metric suite is a class with the *«MetricSuite»* stereotype applied. Both parameter and metric definitions are attributes of the metric suite.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Purpose**

Calculated at regular intervals, metrics help track the evolution of the model development. One metric is the result of a single calculation of a metric definition. The metrics of all the metric definitions of the metric suite, together with relevant parameters, are stored in a single instance of the metric suite as slot values. Use the Metric Table to analyze and manage the instances of the metric suite.

With a help of a Metric Table you can:

- Calculate model metrics.
- Change the parameters of the metrics and recalculate them.
- Customize the representation of the metrics.
- Export the data to an .html , .xlsx , or .csv file.

##### **Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Specific tasks in the Metric Table**

The following links outline the specific procedures when working with the Metric Table:

****

****

## PAGE 00829: Microsoft C++ Profiles

- page_id: `136715618`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715618/Microsoft+C+Profiles
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:38.792+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Our modeling tool provides new profiles [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Microsoft Visual C++ Profile' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='C++/CLI Profile' space='MD2024x'], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='C++ managed profile' space='MD2024x'] to support Microsoft-specific structure and keywords.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Profile Dependency

The*Microsoft Visual C++ Profile* provides stereotypes and tagged values for modeling *Microsoft Visual C++*, which has additional specific keywords and constructs. It conforms to Microsoft Visual C++ 6.0 and later. However, it does not include keywords and constructs related to Managed features.

Microsoft Visual C++ has been called *Managed C++*, Visual Studio 2003. In Visual Studio 2005, the set of keywords and constructs related to Managed features changed and is also known as C++/CLI.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span>Our modeling tool provides new profiles <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Visual C++ Profile" /></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="C++/CLI Profile" /></ac:link>, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="C++ managed profile" /></ac:link> to support <span>Microsoft-specific structure and keywords</span>.<br /></span></p><p><span><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2018-1-24_17-50-9.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft C++ Profiles" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>Profile Dependency </span></h6><p><span>The<em> Microsoft Visual C++ Profile</em> provides stereotypes and tagged values for modeling <em>Microsoft Visual C++</em>, which has additional specific keywords and constructs. It conforms to Microsoft Visual C++ 6.0 and later. However, it does not include keywords and constructs related to Managed features. </span></p><p><span>Microsoft Visual C++ has been called <em>Managed C++</em>, Visual Studio 2003. In Visual Studio 2005, the set of keywords and constructs related to Managed features changed and is also known as C++/CLI. </span></p><p><span><br /></span></p>
````

## PAGE 00830: Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)

- page_id: `136714631`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714631/Microsoft+Office+Excel+worksheet+XLSX
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:44.215+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix B: office open XML format template
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### Multi-Line Statements in *XLSX*

All multi-line directives such as #if, #else, #elseif, #foreach, and #macro must be used under the following conditions.

The beginning and ending statements must be declared within a single cell. The figures below show samples of invalid usage of the **#if** and **#foreach** statements between cells respectively.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Executing multi-line statements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Invalid usage of #foreach statements.

In the first figure, since the body of the #if statement ($e.name) resides in the A2 cell, not in the A1 cell, the body will not be generated when generating a report, regardless of the evaluation of the #if statement. The code shown in the second figure will break the structure of a spreadsheet document.

The following two figures demonstrate samples of valid usage of the #if and #foreach statements respectively.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Valid usage of an #if statement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Valid usage of a #foreach statement.

- A VTL Macro must be declared within a single cell. Do not insert the multi-cell recorded macros in a single cell, see figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Invalid usage of a VTL Macro.

The macro will copy all contents between #macro and #end. Cells and rows will be included in the macro as well. Once this record has been inserted, the macro content will break the document structure.

The following figure demonstrates a sample of valid usage of the #macro statement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Valid usage of a VTL Macro.

#### Creating data for multiple rows

The **#foreach** directive can only be used in a single cell record. To create data for multiple rows, use the **#forrow** directive instead (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The forrow directive.

The figure below demonstrates the output of the above code.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Results of running the code from figure 7.

#### Creating data for multiple columns

**#forcol** is used for creating data for multiple columns (see the figure below). This statement can be used in conjunction with the **#forrow** statement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The forcol directive.

The following figure demonstrates the output of the above code.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Results of running the code from the figure above.

#### Displaying content in a cell

Texts in any generated report are always wrapped. Also, the cells’ width in the generated report depends on the cells’ width in the report template used. For example, an *XLSX* report template in the first figure below will generate an output report as shown in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The forrow directive.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Results of running the code from the figure above.

#### Limitation when used in Microsoft Office Excel worksheet

You cannot use **#sectionBegin** and **#includeSection** in *XLSX*. If you try to use **#sectionBegin** or **#includeSection** in an *XLSX* report template (the first figure below) an error message will open (the second figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Invalid usage of a sectionBegin directive.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The error message from running the code in the figure above.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h2>Multi-Line Statements in <em>XLSX</em></h2><p>All multi-line directives such as #if, #else, #elseif, #foreach, and #macro must be used under the following conditions.</p><p style="text-align: left;">The beginning and ending statements must be declared within a single cell. The figures below show samples of invalid usage of the <strong>#if</strong> and <strong>#foreach</strong> statements between cells respectively.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_6.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Executing multi-line statements.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_7.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Invalid usage of #foreach statements.</h6><p>In the first figure, since the body of the #if statement ($e.name) resides in the A2 cell, not in the A1 cell, the body will not be generated when generating a report, regardless of the evaluation of the #if statement. The code shown in the second figure will break the structure of a spreadsheet document.</p><p style="text-align: left;">The following two figures demonstrate samples of valid usage of the #if and #foreach statements respectively.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_8.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Valid usage of an #if statement.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_9.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Valid usage of a #foreach statement.</h6><ul><li>A VTL Macro must be declared within a single cell. Do not insert the multi-cell recorded macros in a single cell, see figure below.</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_10.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Invalid usage of a VTL Macro.</h6><p>The macro will copy all contents between #macro and #end. Cells and rows will be included in the macro as well. Once this record has been inserted, the macro content will break the document structure.</p><p>The following figure demonstrates a sample of valid usage of the #macro statement.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_11.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Valid usage of a VTL Macro.</h6><h2>Creating data for multiple rows</h2><p style="text-align: left;">The <strong>#foreach</strong> directive can only be used in a single cell record. To create data for multiple rows, use the <strong>#forrow</strong> directive instead (see the following figure).</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_12.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">The forrow directive.</h6><p style="text-align: left;">The figure below demonstrates the output of the above code.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_13.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Results of running the code from figure 7.</h6><h2>Creating data for multiple columns</h2><p><strong>#forcol</strong> is used for creating data for multiple columns (see the figure below). This statement can be used in conjunction with the <strong>#forrow</strong> statement.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_14.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">The forcol directive.</h6><p>The following figure demonstrates the output of the above code.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_15.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Results of running the code from the figure above.</h6><h2>Displaying content in a cell</h2><p>Texts in any generated report are always wrapped. Also, the cells’ width in the generated report depends on the cells’ width in the report template used. For example, an <em>XLSX</em> report template in the first figure below will generate an output report as shown in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Wrapped Text (Column B) in an XLSX Report Template" ac:alt="Sample of Wrapped Text (Column B) in an XLSX Report Template"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_16.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">The forrow directive.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:title="Wrapping Text Output in XLSX" ac:alt="Wrapping Text Output in XLSX"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_17.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Results of running the code from the figure above.</h6><h2>Limitation when used in Microsoft Office Excel worksheet</h2><p>You cannot use <strong>#sectionBegin</strong> and <strong>#includeSection</strong> in <em>XLSX</em>. If you try to use <strong>#sectionBegin</strong> or <strong>#includeSection</strong> in an <em>XLSX</em> report template (the first figure below) an error message will open (the second figure below).</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_18.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Invalid usage of a sectionBegin directive.</h6><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 14- The error message from running the code in figure 13..png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Excel worksheet (XLSX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">The error message from running the code in the figure above.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714647 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00831: Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)

- page_id: `136714647`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714647/Microsoft+Office+PowerPoint+presentation+PPTX
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:44.627+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix B: office open XML format template
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A presentation document requires a special document template. This template does not contain any content order, and all text content is always placed inside a text box. A text box is an image structure (an image structure keeps the position of each image in x, y coordinates). You can change a Text box position. You can also place a Text box in the same positions as others (see the figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A sample of PPTX template.

#### Multi-line statements in PPTX

Similar to *XLSX*, all multi-line directives such as #if, #else, #elseif, #foreach, and #macro must be used under the following conditions.

1. The beginning and ending statements must be declared within a single text box. The following code shows a sample of invalid usage of the#foreach statement between the text boxes. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Since the PPTX template does not provide the statement order, the template will not be interpreted as the order of the displayed images (text boxes). For example, $uc.name may not be processed after #foreach($uc in $UseCase) has been completely processed. The following code demonstrates a sample of valid usage of the #foreach statement. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. A VTL Macro must be declared within a single text box. Do not insert the multi-cell recorded macros in a single text box. See the following code example. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Since each text box does not have any sequence order, the macro cannot record any content between the text boxes. The following code example demonstrates a valid usage of the #macro statement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Creating data for multiple slides

In PPTX report templates, you can use the #forpage directive to create additional slide(s) in your presentation. You can use #forpage and #endpage directives in any text box. However, the #endpage directive must be on same slide as the #forpage directive or on the following slide, but not before the slide which contains the #forpage directive. All directives on the slide(s) between 1) the slide the #forpage directive appears and 2) the slide the #endpage directive appears will be included within the #forpage statement. For example, the template in the first figure below will produce the output as shown in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Creating data for multiple pages using the forpage directive.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Results of running the code from the figure above.

#### Creating a page with conditions

Since a directive in the *PPTX* report template does not provide any statement order (unless residing in the same text box), you cannot used the #if directive together with the #forpage directive. See figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The code above will not produce the output report as expected, because the #forpage directive automatically covers all directives in the current page regardless of the statement order. Consequently, the #if directive may not be interpreted after the #forpage directive.

To avoid this problem, you can use the $report.filterElement($elements, $types) method. This helper method provides the element filter for the specified type. In this case, use the following code.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

For more details on $report.filterElement($elements, $types), see Section *[Helper Modules](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Helper+tools)*.

#### Limitation when used in Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation

**#sectionBegin**, **#includeSection** cannot be used in any *PPTX* report template. If you try, for example, to use **#sectionBegin** in a *PPTX* report template, an error message (the figure below) will open.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The error message dialog that appears from running an invalid sectionBegin directive.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

A text box is an image structure (an image structure keeps the position of each image in x, y coordinates). You can change a Text box position. You can also place a Text box in the same positions as others (see the figure below).</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of PPTX Template" ac:alt="Sample of PPTX Template"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_20.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">A sample of PPTX template.</h6><h2>Multi-line statements in PPTX</h2><p>Similar to <em>XLSX</em>, all multi-line directives such as #if, #else, #elseif, #foreach, and #macro must be used under the following conditions.</p><ol><li><p>The beginning and ending statements must be declared within a single text box. The following code shows a sample of invalid usage of the#foreach statement between the text boxes.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_21.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="adb06cf4-2d0c-48ff-a8d3-82434924a373"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Since the PPTX template does not provide the statement order, the template will not be interpreted as the order of the displayed images (text boxes). The following code example demonstrates a valid usage of the #macro statement.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_24.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Creating data for multiple slides</h2><p>In PPTX report templates, you can use the #forpage directive to create additional slide(s) in your presentation. You can use #forpage and #endpage directives in any text box. However, the #endpage directive must be on same slide as the #forpage directive or on the following slide, but not before the slide which contains the #forpage directive. All directives on the slide(s) between 1) the slide the #forpage directive appears and 2) the slide the #endpage directive appears will be included within the #forpage statement. For example, the template in the first figure below will produce the output as shown in the second figure below.<br /> </p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_25.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Creating data for multiple pages using the forpage directive.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_26.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Results of running the code from the figure above.</h6><h2>Creating a page with conditions</h2><p>Since a directive in the <em>PPTX</em> report template does not provide any statement order (unless residing in the same text box), you cannot used the #if directive together with the #forpage directive. See figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Looping With Condition in General Style" ac:alt="Sample of Looping With Condition in General Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_27.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The code above will not produce the output report as expected, because the #forpage directive automatically covers all directives in the current page regardless of the statement order. Consequently, the #if directive may not be interpreted after the #forpage directive.</p><p>To avoid this problem, you can use the $report.filterElement($elements, $types) method. This helper method provides the element filter for the specified type. In this case, use the following code.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Looping With Conditional Filter" ac:alt="Sample of Looping With Conditional Filter"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_28.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>For more details on $report.filterElement($elements, $types), see Section <em><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Helper+tools">Helper Modules</a></em>.</p><h2>Limitation when used in Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation</h2><p><strong>#sectionBegin</strong>, <strong>#includeSection</strong> cannot be used in any <em>PPTX</em> report template. If you try, for example, to use <strong>#sectionBegin</strong> in a <em>PPTX</em> report template, an error message (the figure below) will open.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_29.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"> </p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 4- The error message dialog that appears from running an invalid sectionBegin directive..png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">The error message dialog that appears from running an invalid sectionBegin directive.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714615 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00832: Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)

- page_id: `136714615`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714615/Microsoft+Office+Word+document+DOCX
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:43.793+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix B: office open XML format template
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Report Wizard supports most *DOCX* features. You can place the *VTL* codes inside core (properties) and content of any *DOCX* file. All syntax usable in *RTF* can also be used in *DOCX*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Sample of DOCX Converted from an RTF Document.

#### Limitations when used in Microsoft Office Word document

You cannot use multi-line statements in different objects. If you try to use them in*DOCX*, an error message will open. See the following example.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Error Message of Invalid Usage of the Multi-line Statements in DOCX.

#### Creating data for multiple columns

**#forcol** is used for creating data for multiple columns in a row. This statement must be defined in table and can be used in conjunction with the **#forrow** statement. For example:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Sample elements in the MagicDraw Containment tree.

**Template Code:**

| Name | #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol |
| --- | --- |

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

**#forcol** may create different number of columns in each row. See the example below.

**Template Code:**

| #forrow($p in $sPackage) $p.name | #forcol($o in $sorter.sort($p.ownedElement))$o.name#endcol#endrow |
| --- | --- |

**Output:**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

**#forcol** must be defined as the first statement in a column because it is parsed and processed before other statements in the column except **#forrow**. See the example below.

**Template Code**

| Name | #set($p = $Package) #forcol($ap in $p)$ap.name#endcol |
| --- | --- |

**Above code will be parsed to**

| Name | #forcol($ap in $p) #set($p = $Package)$ap.name#endcol |
| --- | --- |

**Output:**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

So, the template code should be:

Template Code:

| Name#set($p = $Package) | #forcol($ap in $p) $ap.name#endcol |
| --- | --- |

**Output:**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**#forcol** will create dynamic columns in a row. This directive does not create columns for the whole table. See the example below.

**Template Code:**

| Name | #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol |
| --- | --- |
|  |  |

**Output:**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Docx supports at most 63 columns per row (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Docx supports at most 63 columns per row.

#### Creating merged column horizontally for DOCX

ReportWizard provides utility functions for creating table properties. The functions of this module are accessible from templates through **$tableprop**. In this version, ReportWizard provides only **$tableprop.mergeColumns()** to create merged columns horizontally in *DOCX* template.

This statement must be defined in table.

****

**1. $tableprop.mergeColumns(int number)**

Merging columns in specified number

Where the parameter is:

- number - the number of columns to merge. This value has to be of type integer and start from 2.

For example:

**Template Code:**

| Project | $tableprop.mergeColumns($Package.size())Packages |
| --- | --- |
| #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol |  |

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

**2. $tableprop.mergeColumns(String stringNumber)**

Merging columns in specified number as String

Where the parameter is:

- stringNumber - the string value of number of columns to be merged. This value has to be of type integer string and start from “2”.

For example:

**Template Code:**

| Project | $tableprop.mergeColumns(“3”) Packages |
| --- | --- |
| #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol |  |

**Output:**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**$table.mergeColumns()** must be defined as the first statement in the column because it is parsed and processed before other statements in column expect **#forrow** and **#forcol.** See the example below.

****

**Template Code:**

| Project | #set($gValue = $Package.size() +1)$tableprop.mergeColumns($gValue)Packages |
| --- | --- |
| #forcol($p in $Package) $p.name#endcol | No Package |

Above code will be parsed to

| Project | $tableprop.mergeColumns($gValue)#set($gValue = $Package.size() + 1)Packages |
| --- | --- |
| #forcol($p in $Package) $p.name#endcol | No Package |

**Output:**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

So, the template code should be:

**Template Code:**

| Project#set($gValue = $Package.size() + 1) | $tableprop.mergeColumns($gValue)Packages |
| --- | --- |
| #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol | No Package |

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**$tableprop.mergecolumns()** will make a column to be a merged column, it does not change the number of columns in a row. See the following example.

****

**Template Code:**

| $tableprop.mergeColumns(“5”)Packages |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol |  |  |

**Output:**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The number of columns in the first row is still 3.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Report Wizard supports most <em>DOCX</em> features. You can place the <em>VTL</em> codes inside core (properties) and content of any <em>DOCX</em> file. All syntax usable in <em>RTF</em> can also be used in <em>DOCX</em>.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="A Sample of DOCX Converted from an RTF Document" ac:alt="A Sample of DOCX Converted from an RTF Document"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">A Sample of DOCX Converted from an RTF Document.</h6><h2>Limitations when used in Microsoft Office Word document</h2><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">You cannot use multi-line statements in different objects. If you try to use them in </span><em>DOCX</em><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">, an error message will open. See the following example.</span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:title="Invalid Usage of Multi-line Statement in DOCX" ac:alt="Invalid Usage of Multi-line Statement in DOCX"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ooxml_4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="message_export.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Error Message of Invalid Usage of the Multi-line Statements in DOCX.</h6><h2>Creating data for multiple columns</h2><p><strong>#forcol</strong> is used for creating data for multiple columns in a row. This statement must be defined in table and can be used in conjunction with the <strong>#forrow</strong> statement. For example:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Sample elements in MagicDraw Containment Tree" ac:alt="Sample elements in MagicDraw Containment Tree"><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Sample elements in the MagicDraw Containment tree.</h6><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><pre>Name</pre></td><td><pre> #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol</pre></td></tr></tbody></table><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b228f395-e33f-4df1-891f-3c06c3e58988"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>#forcol</strong> may create different number of columns in each row. See the example below.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td>#forrow($<span style="color: rgb(0,51,102);">p</span> in $sPackage) $p.name</td><td>#forcol($o in $sorter.sort($p.ownedElement))<br />$o.name#endcol#endrow</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-3.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></p><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="66ec4a0d-2a3a-4106-af12-75e51f6dc928"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>#forcol</strong> must be defined as the first statement in a column because it is parsed and processed before other statements in the column except <strong>#forrow</strong>. See the example below.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Template Code</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td>Name</td><td>#set($p = $Package) #forcol($ap in $p)<br />$ap.name#endcol</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Above code will be parsed to </strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td>Name</td><td>#forcol($ap in $p) #set($p = $Package)<br />$ap.name#endcol</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-4.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>So, the template code should be:</p><p>Template Code:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td>Name#set($p = $Package)</td><td>#forcol($ap in $p) $ap.name#endcol</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-5.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40a9cd37-ed82-4ed0-b604-1844c3293d67"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>#forcol</strong> will create dynamic columns in a row. This directive does not create columns for the whole table. See the example below.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><tbody><tr><td><pre>Name</pre></td><td><pre> #forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol</pre></td></tr><tr><td> </td><td> </td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-6.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2ef7bfbe-b319-46e0-ae7d-49e2de9677a7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Docx supports at most 63 columns per row (see the following figure).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Docx supports at most 63 columns per row" ac:alt="Docx supports at most 63 columns per row"><ri:attachment ri:filename="section-26-1-2-diagram-five.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Docx supports at most 63 columns per row.</h6><h2>Creating merged column horizontally for DOCX</h2><p>ReportWizard provides utility functions for creating table properties. The functions of this module are accessible from templates through <strong>$tableprop</strong>. In this version, ReportWizard provides only <strong>$tableprop.mergeColumns()</strong> to create merged columns horizontally in <em>DOCX</em> template.</p><p>This statement must be defined in table.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>1. $tableprop.mergeColumns(int number)</strong></p><p>Merging columns in specified number</p><p>Where the parameter is:</p><ul><li>number - the number of columns to merge. This value has to be of type integer and start from 2.</li></ul><p>For example:</p><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td rowspan="2">Project </td><td>$tableprop.mergeColumns($Package.size())Packages</td></tr><tr><td>#forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol</td></tr></tbody></table><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-8.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>2. $tableprop.mergeColumns(String stringNumber)</strong></p><p>Merging columns in specified number as String</p><p>Where the parameter is:</p><ul><li>stringNumber - the string value of number of columns to be merged. This value has to be of type integer string and start from “2”.</li></ul><p>For example:</p><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td rowspan="2">Project</td><td>$tableprop.mergeColumns(“3”) Packages</td></tr><tr><td>#forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-8.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b860ecce-dc5d-451e-be0e-386a2bf2db24"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>$table.mergeColumns()</strong> must be defined as the first statement in the column because it is parsed and processed before other statements in column expect <strong>#forrow</strong> and <strong>#forcol.</strong> See the example below.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td rowspan="2">Project</td><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="2">#set($gValue = $Package.size() +<br />1)$tableprop.mergeColumns($gValue)Packages</td></tr><tr><td>#forcol($p in $Package) $p.name#endcol</td><td>No Package</td></tr></tbody></table><p>Above code will be parsed to </p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td rowspan="2">Project</td><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="2"> $tableprop.mergeColumns($gValue)<br />#set($gValue = $Package.size() + 1)Packages </td></tr><tr><td>#forcol($p in $Package) $p.name#endcol </td><td>No Package</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>  Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-10.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></p><p> </p><p>So, the template code should be:</p><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td rowspan="2">Project#set($gValue = $Package.size() + 1)</td><td colspan="2"> $tableprop.mergeColumns($gValue)Packages</td></tr><tr><td>#forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol </td><td>No Package</td></tr></tbody></table><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-11.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="319d1535-565b-4779-b0e5-43f2e54601e3"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>$tableprop.mergecolumns()</strong> will make a column to be a merged column, it does not change the number of columns in a row. See the following example.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Template Code:</strong></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td>$tableprop.mergeColumns(“5”)Packages</td><td> </td><td> </td></tr><tr><td>#forcol($p in $Package)$p.name#endcol</td><td> </td><td> </td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Output:</strong></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="forrow-12.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Microsoft Office Word document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The number of columns in the first row is still 3.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715620 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00833: Microsoft Visual C++ Profile

- page_id: `136715620`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715620/Microsoft+Visual+C+Profile
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:39.054+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering > Microsoft C++ Profiles
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Profile Name:**Microsoft Visual C++**

ProfileModule Name:**C++_MS_Profile.xml**

##### Data type

The Microsoft Visual C++ profile includes only the data types that do not exist in the ANSI C++ profile. Microsoft C/C++ supports sized integer types. Declaration of 8-, 16-, 32-, or 64-bit integer variables can be done by using the __intn type specifier, where n is 8, 16, 32, or 64.

The types __int8, __int16, and __int32 are synonyms for the ANSI types that have the same size, and are useful for writing portable code that behaves identically across multiple platforms.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Microsoft Visual C++ Data Type

##### Stereotype

#### NOTE: Profile Table

Profile Table

The profile table and description in this section do not include the tagged value inherited from C++ ANSI profile.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

VC++Class

| --- | --- | --- |
| VC++Class | Class |  |
| Tag | Type | Description |
| inheritance | inheritanceType[0..1] = single (Enumeration) See inheritanceType | Represents the utilization of VC++ keywords, __single_inheritance, __multiple_inheritance, and__virtual_inheritance. |
| abstract | boolean[1] = false | Represents the utilization of keyword __abstract or abstract, depending on C++ dialects. |
| Inherited tag | Type | Description |
| declspecModifier «VC++StorageClass» | String | For keeping an extended declaration modifier of __declspec See Extended storage-class attributes with __declspec |
| C++Attributes «VC++Element» | String | For keeping C++ Attributes |
| «VC++Struct» inherits from «C++Struct» and «VC++StorageClass» |  |  |

| C++Attributes «VC++Element» | String | For keeping C++ Attributes |
| VC++Union |  |  |

| VC++Enumeration |  |  |

| VC++Enumeration | Enumeration |  |
| Tag | Type | Description |
| Type | Classifier[0..1] | Represents the type of enumeration literal |
| VC++Typedef <<VC++Typedef>> inherits from <<C++Typedef>> and <<VC++StorageClass>> |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Constraints |
| VC++Typedef | Class |  |
| Inherited Tag | Type | Description |
| C++Attributes «VC++Element» | String | For keeping C++ Attributes |
| VC++Attribute |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Constraints |
| VC++Attribute | Proeprty |  |
| Inherited Tag | Type | Description |
| C++Attributes «VC++Element» | String | For keeping C++Attributes |
| VC++Operation |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Constraints |
| VC++Operation | Operation |  |
| Tag | Type | Description |
| callingConvention | callingConvention [0..1] (Enumeration) See callingConvention | Represents the utilization of keywords. __cdecl __clrcall __stdcall __fastcall __thiscall |
| __inline | boolean[1] = false | Represents the utilization of the __inline keyword. |
| __forceinline | boolean[1] = false | Represents the utilization of the __forceinline keyword. |
| Inherited Tag | Type | Description |
| declspecModifier «VC++StorageClass» | String | For keeping an extended declaration modifier of __declspecSee Extended storage-class attributes with __declspec |
| C++Attributes «VC++Element» | String | For keeping C++ Attributes |
| VC++Parameter |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Constraints |
| VC++Parameter | Parameter |  |
| Inherited Tag | Type | Description |
| C++Attributes «VC++Element» | String | For keeping C++ Attributes |
| VC++StorageClass«VC++StorageClass» is an abstract stereotype corresponding to extended declaration modifier of __declspec. |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Constraints |
| VC++StorageClass | Element |  |
| Tag | Type | Description |
| declspeModifier | String | For keeping an extended declaration modifier of __declspec. See Extended storage-class attributes with __declspec |
| VC++Element |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Constraints |
| VC++Element | Element |  |
| Tag | Type | Description |
| C++Attributes | String | For keeping C++Attributes |
| VC++Interface |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Description |
| VC++Interface | Interface | Represents interface declaration with __interface keyword. |
| VC++Event |  |  |
| Name | Meta Class | Description |
| VC++Event | Operation, Property | Represents interface, method or member declaration with __event keyword. |
| Tag | Type | Description |
| isVirtualEvent | boolean[1] = false | Supports virtual event as shown in the example below.// data member as event virtual __event ClickEventHandler*OnClick; |

##### Enumeration

inheritanceType

**inheritanceType** will be used as the value of the tag named "**inheritance**" under **«VC++Class»**.

| Literal | Description |
| --- | --- |
| single | Representation of __single_inheritance keyword. |
| multiple | Representation of __multiple_inheritance keyword. |
| virtual | Representation of __virtual_inheritance keyword. |

callingConvention

**callingConvention** will be used as the value of the tag named “**calling convention**” under **«VC++Operation»**

| Literal | Description |
| --- | --- |
| __cdecl | Represents the utilization of __cdecl modifier. |
| __clrcall | Represents the utilization of __clrcall modifier. |
| __stdcall | Represents the utilization of __stdcall modifier. |
| __fastcall | Represents the utilization of __fastcall modifier. |
| __thiscall | Represents the utilization of __thiscall modifier. |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136713462 space=MD2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00834: Migrating projects to newer version

- page_id: `136713462`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136713462/Migrating+projects+to+newer+version
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-01-13T05:31:26.608+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Collaboration powered by 3DEXPERIENCE platform
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Important note on versions

1053053858

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Important note on versions

1053053878

INLINE

1053053856

INLINE

**On this page**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Important note on versions

1053053868

INLINE

After upgrading your modeling tool, you need to migrate your projects to the newer version. Because [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding standard profiles to the 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space=''] and stored in the **3D**EXPERIENCE platform, just as other projects, you will also need to migrate standard profiles after upgrading your modeling tool. There are a few ways to do this.

##### Migrating projects and standard profiles all at once

You can migrate projects and standard profiles all at once.

To migrate all projects at once

1. Start the modeling tool and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logging in to 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='']log in to **3D**EXPERIENCE platform .
2. Select Collaborate > Migrate Projects to Version <version number>.
3. In the drop-down menu on the right side of the dialog select **All Projects.**You will see a list of all projects and standard profiles.**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
4. To select all projects and standard profiles, click the Select All/Clear All button on the left side of the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='migrate_all_projects_select_all.png']
5. Once you have selected the items you want to migrate, click OK.

##### Migrating standard profiles

To migrate projects separately from standard profiles, you must first migrate the standard profiles.

To migrate standard profiles

1. Start the modeling tool and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logging in to 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='']log in to **3D**EXPERIENCE platform . Make sure to select a public collaborative space that everyone can access. To learn how to create a collaborative space for standard profiles, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding standard profiles to the 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space=''].
2. Select Collaborate > Migrate Projects to Version <version number>.
3. In the drop-down menu on the right side of the dialog select **Standard Profiles. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
4. To select all standard profiles, click the Select All/Clear All button on the left side of the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='migrating_standard_profiles_select_all.png']
5. Select the standard profiles you want to migrate and click **OK.**

##### Migrating projects

You can migrate projects either automatically through the dedicated dialog or manually by opening a project that has not yet been migrated. Please note that for either of these methods to work you have to beforehand.

###### Migrating projects automatically

If you migrate projects automatically through the dedicated dialog, all branches of the projects will be migrated.

To migrate projects automatically

1. Start the modeling tool and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logging in to 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='']log in to **3D**EXPERIENCE platform .
2. Select Collaborate > Migrate Projects to Version <version number>.
3. In the drop-down menu on the right side of the dialog select **Projects. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
4. To select all projects, click the Select All/Clear All button on the left side of the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='migrate_projects_select_all.png']
5. Select the projects you want to migrate and click OK.

###### Migrating projects manually

If you choose to migrate a project that you are opening, only the branch that you are opening will be migrated.

To migrate a project manually

1. Start the modeling tool and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logging in to 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='']log in to **3D**EXPERIENCE platform .
2. On the main menu, click Collaborate > Projects . The Manage projects dialog opens.
3. Select a project and click Open . A dialog prompts you to update the System/Standard Profiles in the project to allow project editing. [ATTACHMENT filename='incompatible_system_profiles.png']
4. Click Continue . After the System/Standard Profiles are updated, the modeling tool opens a notification showing that the model was successfully updated. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
You can also see the same notification in the**Notification Window**. You can open the **Notification Window** by pressing Ctrl + M or clicking **Window** > **Notification Window.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Important note on versions

Important note on versions

- If your platform is upgraded to a newer version, but you are still using the same version of a modeling tool , standard profiles do not change and you do not need to migrate them.
- When you migrate a standard profile, you [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating revisions and branches' space=''] in the platform for each migrated version.
- You can only migrate projects to a newer version of the modeling tool. For example, if you are working with the 2022x Refresh1 version and want to migrate to 2022x Refresh2, you can migrate projects partially to allow users to continue working on both 2022x Refresh1 and 2022x Refresh2 versions. However, if you have already started working and creating projects on a newer version, you cannot migrate or add projects to an older version.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136727587 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00835: Missing expression language

- page_id: `136727587`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136727587/Missing+expression+language
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2023-09-22T06:46:53.049+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The modeling tool supports multiple [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported scripting languages' space=''] you can use for expression specification. Additionally, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported scripting languages' space=''] you may need. However, if your project contains expressions that use a scripting language that is neither built-in nor manually added to the tool, it is considered missing in the tool; thus, the specified expressions cannot be executed, causing errors in the project.

##### Expression evaluation failure

For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed. Since, in this case, JavaScript Nashorn is neither built-in nor added manually to the modeling tool, the script cannot be executed, causing an evaluation error, as shown below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The error notification indicates the element containing the failed expression. Clicking the icon on the bottom right opens a Notification Window that displays the element name with the script that cannot be executed.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Expression evaluation failure notification in the Notification Window

The missing language is also indicated in the Expression dialog's Language field for scripts.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Missing language indication in the Expression dialog

##### Resolving expression evaluation failure

To resolve an expression evaluation failure, do one of the following

- Rewrite the script in a scripting language that is already supported by the modeling tool. To learn more about the built-in languages, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported scripting languages' space=''] page.
- Add the missing language to the modeling tool. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a scripting language to MagicDraw' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The modeling tool supports multiple <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported scripting languages" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[built-in scripting languages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you can use for expression specification. Additionally, you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported scripting languages" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[add other scripting languages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you may need. However, if your project contains expressions that use a scripting language that is neither built-in nor manually added to the tool, it is considered missing in the tool; thus, the specified expressions cannot be executed, causing errors in the project.</p><h3>Expression evaluation failure</h3><p>For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed. Since, in this case, JavaScript Nashorn is neither built-in nor added manually to the modeling tool, the script cannot be executed, causing an evaluation error, as shown below. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="expression_evaluation_error.png" /></ac:image> </h6><p>The error notification indicates the <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">element containing the failed expression</span>. Clicking the icon on the bottom right opens a Notification Window that displays the element name with the script that cannot be executed. </p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="notification_window_expression_evaluation_error.png" /></ac:image>Expression evaluation failure notification in the Notification Window</h6><p>The missing language is also indicated in the Expression dialog's Language field for scripts.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="missing_language_expression_dialog.png" /></ac:image>Missing language indication in the Expression dialog</h6><h3>Resolving expression evaluation failure</h3><p>To resolve an expression evaluation failure, do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Rewrite the script in a scripting language that is already supported by the modeling tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6ea17ebc-15ef-460f-b0f5-cbbe38882859"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To learn more about the built-in languages, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported scripting languages" /></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Add the missing language to the modeling tool. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de6f0ee7-9c62-4a58-b0b8-c4c1f29387c3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a scripting language to MagicDraw" /></ac:link> page. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711180 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00836: Model

- page_id: `136711180`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711180/Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:26.665+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A model contains a (hierarchical) set of elements that describes the physical system being modeled. It can also contain a set of elements that represent the environment of the system (typically [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024x']together with their interrelationships), such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024x']and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space='MD2024x'].

A model is presented as a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MD2024x']with a small triangle in the upper right corner of the large rectangle. The triangle can be shown in the tab.

The model is defined as a package; that is, it has package properties in the Model [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'], as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Object diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>A model contains a (hierarchical) set of elements that describes the physical system being modeled. It can also contain a set of elements that represent the environment of the system (typically <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Actor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Actors ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>together with their interrelationships), such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Association" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Associations ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Dependencies]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>A model is presented as a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[package ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>with a small triangle in the upper right corner of the large rectangle. The triangle can be shown in the tab.</p><p>The model is defined as a package; that is, it has package properties in the Model <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, as shown below.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Model Specification window" ac:alt="Model Specification window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_specification.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bcb055ec-bcd9-463c-97e8-112f9e8d79a2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Object diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Actor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Actors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Package" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Association" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Associations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709204 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00838: Model Browser

- page_id: `136709204`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709204/Model+Browser
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T15:08:33.112+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > MagicDraw getting started > Understanding the user interface
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1421916364

1421916368

1421916367

INLINE

Model Browser provides a visual representation of the hierarchy of your model elements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The items in the Model Browser hierarchy can be:

- Compressed – a [IMAGE alt='' src=''] plus sign next to an icon indicates that the icon is compressed. This is the default setting. It means that the element contains other model elements. Click the plus sign to expand the icon and view its subordinate items.
- Expanded – a [IMAGE alt='' src=''] minus sign next to an icon indicates that the icon is fully expanded. Click the minus sign to collapse the item.

If there is no plus or minus sign next to an icon, it does not contain other model elements.

The Model Browser is a hierarchical navigation tool that allows you to manage your model data, including packages, components, classes, all UML diagrams, extension mechanisms, and other data. The Model Browser may be used as an alternative tool to the MagicDraw menus and toolbars. It is easier to work with project diagrams and data elements using the Model Browser.

You can perform the following operations in the Model Browser:

- Create and specify model elements.
- Copy, cut, and paste model elements.
- Delete model elements.
- Drag model elements to the Diagram pane and inside the Model Browser.
- Drag data in the Code engineering sets (you may create data in the root package *Model* branch, drag it to the Code Engineering sets, and then the round trip object is created automatically).
- View the hierarchy of all model elements.
- Create a symbol for the selected model element in the current diagram.
- Manage diagrams.
- Manage extension mechanisms, such as constraints, stereotypes, and tagged values.
- Java reverse of a class from the classpath.
- Adjust the code engineering sets.
- Generate code for particular sets.
- Filter visible items by any model type (for example, class, package, operation, component, state, and others - for both views and dates).
- Sort the visible items of the selected model element.
- Sort all model elements.
- Display search results.
- Display locked elements in the server project.

The Model Browser window has the following tabs**:**

- **Containment** tab groups data into logical sets.
- **Structure** tab represents deeply nested structures of thestructuredclassifier.
- **Inheritance** tab represents the model hierarchy of the project.
- **Diagrams** tab groups diagrams according to the diagrams type or shows them as a list.
- **Model Extensions** tab represents all predefined and created constraints and stereotypes.
- **Search Results** tab displays search results.

The **Search Results** tab is not displayed by default. It appears after you perform the search.

- **Lock View**tab represents locked elements of the server project.

Lock View

1421916363

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709253 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00839: Model Extensions tab

- page_id: `136709253`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709253/Model+Extensions+tab
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:11:39.493+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > MagicDraw getting started > Understanding the user interface > Model Browser
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1425754140

1425754143

1425754142

INLINE

The Model Extensions tab contains all Stereotypes that are predefined and created manually in the project. In this tree, you can create, review, copy/paste, and delete extension mechanisms.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To open the Model Extensions tab, do one of the following

- At the top of the Model Browser, click the Model Extensions tab.
- If the Model Extensions tab is hidden, from the **Window** menu, select **Model Extensions** You can change some Model Extensions tab properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space='MD2024x']**Environment Options** dialog in the **Browser** options group.

Model Extensions tab consists of the following two components:

- Model Extensions tab toolbar
- Model Extensions tree

##### Model Extensions tab toolbar

**Model Extensions** tab toolbar contains the following buttons:

| Button | Tooltip text | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Collapse All | Collapses all of the current elements in the Containment tree. |
|  | Collapse Selected Recursively | Collapses only selected node and all subnodes in that node. |
|  | Group by Profiles | Groups extensions by the profiles. |
|  | Group by Metaclasses | Groups extensions by the metaclasses. |
|  | Favorites | Opens the Favorites menu for choosing to do one of the following:Add a selected element to favoritesOpen the dialog for managing favoritesNavigate to a desired favorite in the Containment tree |
|  | Quick Find | Opens the Quick Find dialog. |

The same actions you may perform using the shortcut menu of the Model Extensions tree.

##### Working with model elements in the Model Extensions tree

You can work with elements in the Model Extension tree using the commands available from the selected element's shortcut menu.

For more information about commands available from the shortcut menu in the Model Browser, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements in the Model Browser' space='MD2024x'].

1425754139
