<strong>Template Editor</strong>, see figure above, or</li><li>On the Microsoft Word 2007 menu, click <strong>Add-Ins</strong> &gt; <strong>ReportWizard</strong> &gt; <strong>Template Editor</strong>, see figure below.</li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"> <ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Microsoft Word 2007 Template Editor Menu" ac:alt="Microsoft Word 2007 Template Editor Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Microsoft Word 2007 Template Editor Menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Opening template editor" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Microsoft Word 2007 Template Editor Menu.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e53351d-e0f3-4fb7-8fee-21349b4e3639"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The macro-enabled option in Microsoft Word is required to open Template Editor.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>When Template Editor is open, the Report Wizard Template Editor dialog will open.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Report Wizard Template Editor Dialog" ac:alt="Report Wizard Template Editor Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Template Editor Dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Opening template editor" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Report Wizard Template Editor Dialog.</h6><p>The table below shows the Report Wizard Template Editor Options</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Name </th><th>Function </th></tr><tr><td>Search box</td><td>Filter a list of fields. Only fields <br />that contain a keyword of search <br />as part of their names can be <br />shown in the (4) <strong>List of fields</strong>. </td></tr><tr><td>Clear search <br />results</td><td>Clear the current search result.</td></tr><tr><td>Categories combo <br />box</td><td>Select categories of fields. <br />Fields are shown in (4) <br /><strong>List of fields</strong> according to their <br />categories.</td></tr><tr><td>List of fields</td><td>Show a list of fields. Double-click <br />a field name to insert the code.</td></tr><tr><td>Display all available <br />fields check box</td><td>Show all fields, otherwise it will <br />show only commonly used fields.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Insert <br />button</td><td colspan="1">Insert a code for a selected <br />field into the document.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Show/Hide description <br />button</td><td colspan="1">Show or hide (8) <br /><strong>description pane</strong>.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Description <br />pane</td><td colspan="1">Show the description of a <br />selected field.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Import button</td><td colspan="1">Import a data file.</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710937 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00916: Operation

- page_id: `136710937`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710937/Operation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:07.440+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Class
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1778044973

1778044987

1778044977

Entries in the operation compartment are strings that show the operations defined on classes as well as those that are supplied by the classes. An operation is a service that can be requested to perform by an instance of the class. It has a name and a list of arguments.

Usually, class attributes are accessed through the operations. The operations are used to perform specific actions, such as system calls, utility functions, and queries. The operation signature provides all information needed to use that operation.

You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024x'] in the operation [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']. In the same window, you can find the description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window.

To create a new operation, do one of the following

- On the diagram, select the class, click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart manipulation' space='MD2024x'] > Operation .
- In the class Specification window > Operations property group, click the Create button.
- In the Containment tree, from the class shortcut menu, select Create Element > Operation .
- On the diagram, select the class and press the Ctrl+Alt+O.

To define the type of an operation, do one of the following

- Select an operation, type ":" and then specify the type. If you add a non-existent type, a new class is created for that type. After typing the ":", press Ctrl+Space or Ctrl+Backspace to get a list of suggested types.
- In the Operation Specification window, the Type property, select the operation type.

To quickly create a behavior or a diagram for an operation

1. From the operation shortcut menu, select Create Method and either Behavior , or Diagram.
2. Select one of either behaviors or diagrams you wish to create.

To add an operation parameter, do one of the following

- In the Operation Specification window, Parameters property group, click the Create button.
- On the diagram, select an operation and type a parameter text in parenthesis.
- Select an operation in the Containment tree and for the shortcut menu, select Create Element > Parameter .

1778044972

**Related Pages**

- Model Elements
- Specification Window
- Parameter

## PAGE 00918: Operation Language Properties

- page_id: `136715556`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715556/Operation+Language+Properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:34.710+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering > Conversion from an Earlier Project Version > Language Properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This example isOperationClassclass that has operation namedOperationClass()which is constructor andoperation named myOperation. The Model that is being shown in the figure below is a translation. There are Inline modifier and Virtual modifier in Operation Language Properties that need to be translated and apply the «C++Operation» stereotype. There are Initialization list and Explicit modifier in Operation Language Properties that need to be translated and apply the «C++Constructor» stereotype.

#### INFO: Constructor

Constructor

Initialization list and Explicit modifier will be translated when it was set in Constructor. The Constructor is an operation that has the same name as its owner or applies the «constructor» stereotype in UML Standard Profile.

| Operation - Initialization List |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old Value | Translation |
| <empty> | no change |
| myAttribute(n) | Apply the «C++Constructor» stereotype and set initialization list tag value to myAttribute(n). |
|  |  |
|  |  |
|  |  |

| Operation - Inline Modifier |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old Value | Translation |
| not inline | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype and set inline tag value to false. |
| inline | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype and set inline tag value to true. |
|  |  |
|  |  |
|  |  |

| Operation - Virtual Modifier |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old Value | Translation |
| not virtual | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype and set virtual tag value to false. |
| virtual | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype and set virtual tag value to true. |
|  |  |

| Operation - Explicit Modifier |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old Value | Translation |
| not explicit | Apply the «C++Constructor» stereotype and set explicit tag value to false. |
| explicit | Apply the «C++Constructor» stereotype and set explicit tag value to true. |
|  |  |

##### Operation - Return value changeability

This Example isReturnValueChangeabilityClassclass that has three operations and all operations have setreturn value changeability in Language Properties to const as below.

###### Figure 68 -- Return value changeability Example in Class Diagram

The Model that is being shown in the figure below is a translation.

###### Figure 69 -- Translated Return value changeability in Class Diagram

The Return value changeability Language Properties is being shown in the figure below.

| Old value | Translation |
| --- | --- |
| none | no change. |
| const, operation doesn’t has parameter. | Create one return type parameter and set Type Modifier to const $. |
| const, operation has parameter but does not have return type parameter. | Create one return type parameter and set Type Modifier to const $. |
| const, operation has return type parameter. | Set Type Modifier in return type parameter to const $. |

##### Attribute

This example isAttributeClassclass that has attribute namedmyAttribute, return type isintand type modifier is [15].

Figure 71 -- Attribute Example in Class Diagram

The Model that is being shown in the figure below is translation.

Figure 72 -- Translated Attribute in Class Diagram

There areMutable,Bit field,Abbreviated InitializerandContainerin Attribute Language Properties that need to be translated and apply the«C++Attribute» stereotype. AVolatilein Attribute Language Properties will move toType Modifier.

Figure 73 -- Attribute Language Properties

Figure 74 -- Attribute Mutable

| Attribute - Mutable |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old value | Translation |
| false | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set mutable tag value to false. |
| true | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set mutable tag value to true. |

Figure 75 -- Attribute Volatile

| Attribute - Volatile |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old value | Translation |
| false | no change. |
| true | Set Type Modifier to volatile $. |

Figure 76 -- Attribute Bit field

| Attribute - Bit field |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old value | Translation |
| <empty> | no change. |
| 4 | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set bit field tag value to 4. |

Figure 77 -- Attribute Abbreviated Initializer

| Attribute - Abbreviated initializer |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old value | Translation |
| false | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set abbreviated initialization tag value to false. |
| true | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set abbreviated initialization tag value to true. |

Figure 78 -- Attribute Pointer to function

| Attribute - Pointer to function |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old value | Translation |
| false | no change. |
| true | no change. |

Figure 79 -- Attribute Container

| Attribute - Container |  |
| --- | --- |
| Old value | Translation |
| <empty> | no change. |
| vector<T> | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set container tag value to vector<T>. |
| list<T> | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set container tag value to list<T>. |
| map<Key, T, Compare> | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set container tag value to map<Key, T, Compare>. |
| stack<T> | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set container tag value to stack<T>. |
| multimap<Key, T, Compare> | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set container tag value to multimap<Key, T, Com- pare>. |
| set<Key, Compare> | Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype and set container tag value to set<Key, Compare>. |

colspan="1"><pre>const, operation has parameter but </pre><pre>does not have return type parameter.</pre></td><td colspan="1"><span>Create one return type parameter and set </span><span>Type Modifier </span><span>to </span><span>const $</span><span>. </span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><pre>const, operation has return type parameter.</pre></td><td colspan="1"><span>Set </span><span>Type Modifier </span><span>in return type parameter to </span><span>const $</span><span>.</span></td></tr></tbody></table><h3><span>Attribute</span></h3><p><span>This example is </span><span>AttributeClass </span><span>class that has attribute named </span><span>myAttribute</span><span>, return type is </span><span>int </span><span>and type modi</span>fier is [15].</p><p><span>Figure 71 -- Attribute Example in Class Diagram </span></p><p><span>The Model that is being shown in the figure below is translation. </span></p><p><span>Figure 72 -- Translated Attribute in Class Diagram </span></p><p><span>There are </span><span>Mutable</span><span>, </span><span>Bit field</span><span>, </span><span>Abbreviated Initializer </span><span>and </span><span>Container </span><span>in Attribute Language Properties that need to be translated and apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype. A </span><span>Volatile </span><span>in Attribute Language Properties will move to </span><span>Type Modifier</span><span>. </span></p><p><span>Figure 73 -- Attribute Language Properties </span></p><p>Figure 74 -- Attribute Mutable</p><p><br /></p><table><colgroup><col style="width: 45.917664%;" /><col style="width: 54.082336%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><td colspan="2">Attribute - Mutable</td></tr><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><pre>false </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>mutable </span><span>tag value to </span><span>false</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>true </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>mutable </span><span>tag value to </span><span>true</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Figure 75 -- Attribute Volatile </span></p><table><colgroup><col style="width: 44.372505%;" /><col style="width: 55.627495%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><td colspan="2">Attribute - Volatile</td></tr><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><pre>false </pre></td><td><p><span>no change. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>true </pre></td><td><p><span>Set </span><span>Type Modifier </span><span>to </span><span>volatile $</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Figure 76 -- Attribute Bit field </span></p><table><colgroup><col style="width: 48.436424%;" /><col style="width: 51.563576%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><td colspan="2">Attribute - Bit field</td></tr><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><pre>&lt;empty&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>no change. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>4 </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>bit field </span><span>tag value to </span><span>4</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Figure 77 -- Attribute Abbreviated Initializer </span></p><table><colgroup><col style="width: 43.5908%;" /><col style="width: 56.4092%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><td colspan="2">Attribute - Abbreviated initializer</td></tr><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><pre>false </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>abbreviated initialization </span><span>tag value to </span><span>false</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>true </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>abbreviated initialization </span><span>tag value to </span><span>true</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Figure 78 -- Attribute Pointer to function </span></p><table><colgroup><col style="width: 44.372505%;" /><col style="width: 55.627495%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><td colspan="2">Attribute - Pointer to function</td></tr><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><pre>false </pre></td><td><p><span>no change. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>true </pre></td><td><p><span>no change. </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Figure 79 -- Attribute Container </span></p><table><colgroup><col style="width: 47.653774%;" /><col style="width: 52.346226%;" /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><td colspan="2">Attribute - Container</td></tr><tr><th><p><span>Old value </span></p></th><th><p><span>Translation </span></p></th></tr><tr><td><pre>&lt;empty&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>no change. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>vector&lt;T&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>container </span><span>tag value to </span><span>vector&lt;T&gt;</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>list&lt;T&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>container </span><span>tag value to </span><span>list&lt;T&gt;</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>map&lt;Key, T, Compare&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>container </span><span>tag value to </span><span>map&lt;Key, T, Compare&gt;</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>stack&lt;T&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>container </span><span>tag value to </span><span>stack&lt;T&gt;</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>multimap&lt;Key, T, Compare&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>container </span><span>tag value to </span><span>multimap&lt;Key, T, Com- pare&gt;</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><pre>set&lt;Key, Compare&gt; </pre></td><td><p><span>Apply the </span><span>«C++Attribute</span><span>» stereotype and set </span><span>container </span><span>tag value to </span><span>set&lt;Key, Compare&gt;</span><span>. </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715324 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00919: Oracle DDL generation and customization

- page_id: `136715324`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715324/Oracle+DDL+generation+and+customization
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:22.540+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Oracle DDL script generation is based on the Velocity engine. It provides an ability to change the generated DDL script by changing the velocity template. We explain how the Oracle DDL generation works in our modeling tools and how to change the template for specific elements.

Knowledge of the Velocity Template Language is necessary for understanding, editing, or creating templates. The Velocity documentation can be downloaded from [http://click.apache.org/docs/velocity/VelocityUsersGuide.pdf](http://click.apache.org/docs/velocity/VelocityUsersGuide.pdf).

For more information about Oracle DDL 11g, see [http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E25054_01/server.1111/e25789/sqllangu.htm](http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E25054_01/server.1111/e25789/sqllangu.htm).

The modeling tool Oracle DDL generation consists of the following three components:

- The Velocity engine. Oracle DDL generation is performed by the Velocity engine. The engine collects context variables and mergers them into a template.
- The template. The template is a user-defined document that provides the Velocity Template Language (VTL) syntax. The VTL syntax is used to manipulate the context variables for generating the text script. The template file can be changed in the CG Properties Editor dialog box (in the Oracle DDL set shortcut menu, choose the Properties command). The default Oracle DDL template is stored in < program installation folder>\ data\DB_engineering\Oracle_template folder.
- Context variables. Context variables are models (retrieved from OpenAPI), code engineering set information, and user-defined variables.

To identify the Oracle object, check applied Stereotypes and Tagged Values.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It provides an ability to change the generated DDL script by changing the velocity template. We explain how the Oracle DDL generation works in our modeling tools and how to change the template for specific elements.</p><p>Knowledge of the Velocity Template Language is necessary for understanding, editing, or creating templates. The Velocity documentation can be downloaded from <a href="http://click.apache.org/docs/velocity/VelocityUsersGuide.pdf">http://click.apache.org/docs/velocity/VelocityUsersGuide.pdf</a>. </p><p>For more information about Oracle DDL 11g, see <a href="http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E25054_01/server.1111/e25789/sqllangu.htm">http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E25054_01/server.1111/e25789/sqllangu.htm</a>.</p><p>The modeling tool Oracle DDL generation consists of the following three components:</p><ul><li> The Velocity engine. Oracle DDL generation is performed by the Velocity engine. The engine collects context variables and mergers them into a template.</li><li>The template. The template is a user-defined document that provides the Velocity Template Language (VTL) syntax. The VTL syntax is used to manipulate the context variables for generating the text script. The template file can be changed in the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">CG Properties Editor </strong>dialog box (in the Oracle DDL set shortcut menu, choose the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Properties </strong>command). The default Oracle DDL template is stored in &lt;<em>program </em><em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">installation folder&gt;\</em><em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">data\DB_engineering\Oracle_template </em>folder.</li><li>Context variables. Context variables are models (retrieved from OpenAPI), code engineering set information, and user-defined variables.</li></ul><p>To identify the Oracle object, check applied Stereotypes and Tagged Values.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="25b2885a-064a-4fc0-bac0-35a022134e4b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="03a84b5e-cf4a-4758-b490-c4d3fa411bf6"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710244 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00920: Organizing rows

- page_id: `136710244`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710244/Organizing+rows
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:33.926+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Basic tasks in tables > Working with rows
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Sorting rows

You can change the order of rows in the following ways:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organizing rows' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organizing rows' space='MD2024x']

sortTo sort rows by a selected column

- Click the header of the column by which you want to sort the rows in the table. A small arrow showing the automatic sort order appears on the column header. Clicking the header once sorts the rows in ascending order. Clicking the header twice sorts the rows in descending order. All the rows in the table are automatically renumbered after sorting. [ATTACHMENT filename='instance_table_column_sort_orders.png']

multisortTo sort rows by multiple columns

- Press Ctrl + click on the first column header you want to sort the rows in the table. Repeat this action for the next column header you want to sort the rows in the table. A minimum of two column headers should be selected to multi-sort rows. A small arrow with a number showing the automatic multi-sort order appears on the column header. All the rows in the table are automatically renumbered after multi-sorting. [ATTACHMENT filename='multi_sort.png']

##### Moving rows up and down

To move a row up or down

1. Select the row.
2. Do either: The selected row, as well as associated rows, are automatically renumbered after moving.
  - On the table toolbar, click the Up or Down button appropriately.
  - Right-click the row and select the Up or Down command appropriately.
  - Press Ctrl+Up Arrow or Ctrl+Down Arrow appropriately.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organizing rows' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

All the rows in the table are automatically renumbered after sorting.<br /><ac:image ac:title="Sort order identification on columns" ac:alt="Sort order identification on columns"><ri:attachment ri:filename="instance_table_column_sort_orders.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Organizing rows" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3578331-63d5-49cb-980f-fad2d53e7d46"><ac:parameter ac:name="">multisort</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To sort rows by multiple columns</p><hr /><ul><li>Press Ctrl + click on the first column header you want to sort the rows in the table. Repeat this action for the next column header you want to sort the rows in the table. A minimum of two column headers should be selected to multi-sort rows. A small arrow with a number showing the automatic multi-sort order appears on the column header. All the rows in the table are automatically renumbered after multi-sorting.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="multi_sort.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Organizing rows" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><p style="text-align: right;"><br /></p><h3>Moving rows up and down</h3><p>To move a row up or down</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the row.</li><li>Do either:<br /><ul><li>On the table toolbar, click the <strong>Up</strong> or <strong>Down</strong> button appropriately.</li><li>Right-click the row and select the <strong>Up</strong> or <strong>Down</strong> command appropriately.</li><li>Press Ctrl+Up Arrow or Ctrl+Down Arrow appropriately.</li></ul>The selected row, as well as associated rows, are automatically renumbered after moving.</li></ol><p style="text-align: right;"><ac:link ac:anchor="top"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Back to Top]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Organizing rows" /></ac:link></p>

## PAGE 00921: Other traceability relations

- page_id: `136712254`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712254/Other+traceability+relations
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:22.142+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Predefined traceability rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

| Property Name | Description | Applied For: | Reference Through: | Value elements type | Example |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Base Classifier, All General Classifiers | The Base Classifier property gathers classifiers from which the current element directly inherits. The All General Classifiers property gathers classifiers from which the current element directly or indirectly inherits. | Classifier | Relationships: Generalization. | Classifier | Example |
| Participates in Activity | The property shows either the activities wherein the classifier is used as an object type, or the activities that are owned by the classifier. | Classifier | Property Chain | Activity | Example |
| Participates in Interaction | The property shows either the interactions wherein the classifier is used as an object type, or the interactions that are owned by the classifier. | Classifier | Property Chain | Interaction | Example |
| Describing Behavior | The property gathers behavior diagrams (state, activity, sequence, communication, and interaction) that describe the use case. | Use Case | Properties: ownedBehavior Tags: Exceptional Flow of Events Diagrams, Basic Flow of Events Diagrams, Alternative Flow of Events Diagrams. | State, Activity, Sequence, Communication, and Interaction Diagrams | Example |
| Specific Classifier | The property shows directly inherited classifiers. | Classifier | Relationships: Generalizations. | Classifier | Example |
| All Specific Classifiers | The property gathers directly or indirectly inherited classifiers. | Classifier | Relationships: Generalization. | Classifier | Example |
| Used Classifier | The property gathers what classifiers are used in the activity as object types, or the classifier that owns the activity. | Activity | Property Chain | Classifier | Example |
| Used Classifier | The property gathers what classifiers are used in the activity as object types, or the classifier that owns the activity. | InterAction | Property Chain | Classi | Example |
| Described Use Case | The property shows the described use cases by behavior or behavior diagram. | State, Activity, Sequence, Communication, and Interaction Diagrams | Properties: ownedBehavior Tags: Exceptional Flow of Events Diagrams, Basic Flow of Events Diagrams, Alternative Flow of Events Diagrams. | Use Case | Example |

## PAGE 00922: Override Specifiers

- page_id: `136715642`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715642/Override+Specifiers
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:40.761+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering > Microsoft C++ Profiles > Modeling with Microsoft Specific Profiles
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

abstract

| Code | MD-UML |
| --- | --- |
| ref class MyAbstractClass abstract { }; | with «C++CLIClass» and tagged value abstract=true |
|  |  |
| Code | MD-UML |
| ref class MyAbstractClass2 { public:virtual void func() abstract;}; | func() with «C++CLIOperation» and tagged value abstract = true and virtual = true |
|  |  |

new

| Code | MD-UML |
| --- | --- |
| ref class MyClassWithNewFunction{ public: virtual void func() new {} }; | func() with «C++CLIOperation» and tagged value overrideSpcifier = new |
|  |  |

override

| Code | MD-UML |
| --- | --- |
| ref class BaseClass { public: virtual void f(); }; ref class SubClass : public BaseClass { public:virtual void f() override {} }; |  |
|  |  |

sealed

| Code | MD-UML |
| --- | --- |
| interface class MyInterface { virtual void f(); virtual void g(); }; ref class MyClass sealed: public MyInterface{public: virtual void f(){}; virtual void g(){}; }; |  |
|  |  |
| interface class MyInterface2 { public: virtual void f();virtual void g();};ref class MyClass2 : MyInterface2 { public: virtual void f() { } virtual void g() sealed { } // sub class cannot override g() }; |  |
|  |  |
